1:
The chapter opens in spring, at the time when kings go out to battle, and the witnesses read its first verse as much for what it conceals as for what it tells. The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott fixes the season: the Hebrew is 'at the time of the return of the year,' that is, in spring, the campaigning weather of 1 Kings 20:22 and 1 Kings 20:26, when military operations, suspended through the winter, were resumed; the Assyrian kings likewise chronicled their habit of yearly expeditions of conquest and plunder, so that for a king to remain in the country was the exception and not the rule. Joab led forth the power of the army — the entire national array, Samuel's fuller account adding that David sent Joab and his servants and all Israel — and wasted the country, or as Samuel words it, wasted the sons of Ammon, before laying siege to Rabbah the capital, the Rabbath Ammon of Amos 1:14 and Jeremiah 49:2.

But the verse's whole weight lies in its quiet parenthesis, 'But David tarried at Jerusalem,' and here the witnesses turn as one to what the Chronicler has deliberately left out. The wesleyan-arminian Adam Clarke observes that after this very verse the parallel place in Samuel relates the whole story of David and Bathsheba and the murder of Uriah, which the compiler of Chronicles passes over, as he designedly does almost everything prejudicial to the character of David. All he states is that while Joab pressed the war against the Ammonites, David tarried at Jerusalem — and in that tarrying the awful transactions took place. Ellicott adds the literary point that in 2 Samuel 11:1 this same remark prepares the way for the account of David's temptation and fall, so that the Chronicler's abrupt reintroduction of David at Rabbah in the next verse plainly presupposes the narrative told in Samuel, and would hardly be intelligible without it.

The puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry draws the moral of the omission. The fuller account stands in 2 Samuel 11 and 2 Samuel 12, and we cannot but remember this siege by the sad token that while Joab besieged Rabbah David fell into his great sin in the matter of Uriah. Had he been abroad with his army he would have been out of the way of that temptation; but, indulging his ease, he fell into uncleanness. Yet Henry finds the Chronicler's reticence instructive: the recording of the sin in Samuel proves the impartiality and fidelity of the sacred writers, while the refusal to repeat it here, where a fair occasion offered, teaches that though there may be just cause to speak of the faults and miscarriages of others, we should not take delight in the repetition of them, nor love to rake in a dunghill. Of the persons or actions we can say no good of, he counsels, we had best say nothing. The witnesses do not divide; together they let a single parenthesis carry the burden of a story the Chronicler will not retell, and Henry closes on the comfort that though the Lord will severely correct the sins of his believing people, he will not leave them in the hands of their enemies.
4:
The scene shifts from Ammon to the Philistine frontier and the last of the giants, and the witnesses read the transition both as sober textual history and as a parable of grace. The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott begins by marking what the Chronicler has left out: the account with which the parallel section opens in 2 Samuel 21:15-17, telling how David was all but slain by the giant Ishbi-benob, is here dropped, whether by accident or design. The phrase rendered 'there arose war' is literally 'there stood war,' a unique expression that perhaps originated in a misreading of Samuel's 'there became again' at 2 Samuel 21:18. The battle-site Gezer stands where Samuel has the unknown Gob; each word has three consonants in Hebrew, the first shared and the others similar enough to make corruption easy, and though the Syriac and Arabic read 'Gaza,' Ellicott judges Gezer — the town of Joshua 16:3, and the reading of the Septuagint and Vulgate — to be right. Sibbechai the Hushathite, named already in 1 Chronicles 11:29 and 1 Chronicles 27:11, slays Sippai, whom Samuel calls Saph, one of the offspring — a special term, yĕlîdê, as in Numbers 13:22 and Joshua 15:14 — of the Rephaites, Rapha being the collective tribal name of the gigantic Rephaim of Genesis 14:5. The closing note, 'and they were subdued,' he observes, is the Chronicler's own addition.

The puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry lifts the same subjugation into allegory. The Philistines had been nearly subdued already, but as in the conquest of Canaan the sons of Anak were the last to fall in Joshua 11:21, so here the giants of Gath are brought down last of all; and in the conflict between grace and corruption there are some sins which, like these giants, keep their ground a great while and are not mastered without a long struggle, yet judgment is brought forth to victory at last. He marks that we never read of giants among the Israelites as among the Philistines — giants of Gath, but not giants of Jerusalem — for the growth of God's plants is in usefulness, not in bulk, and in the balance of the sanctuary David far outweighs Goliath. David's servants, men of ordinary stature, proved too hard for the giants because they had God on their side, who takes pleasure in abasing lofty looks; the church's friends need not be disheartened by the power and pride of the church's enemies, for we need not fear great men against us while we have the great God for us. These giants, Henry adds, defied Israel and so were marked for ruin, since God will do great things rather than suffer the enemy to behave proudly, as Deuteronomy 32:27 warns. The victories of the Son of David, like David's own, are gradual: we see not yet all things put under him, but it will be seen shortly, when death itself, the last enemy, like these giants, is triumphed over. The witnesses do not contend; Ellicott secures the ground of fact and Henry the height of its meaning.
2:
With Rabbah fallen, David takes the crown of its king, and the witnesses divide gently over whose crown it was and how so vast a thing came to rest on a mortal head. The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott argues that 'the crown of their king' is better read 'the crown of Milcom,' or Moloch, their god: the Hebrew malkâm, 'their Melech,' bears this sense in Zephaniah 1:5 and Amos 5:26, even as the same royal title is elsewhere applied to Jehovah himself in Isaiah 44:6. The Septuagint's 'Molchom, their king,' the Vulgate's 'Melchom,' and the Arabic's 'Malcha, their god' all confirm the reading. And the point is not merely philological, for the crown weighed a talent of gold, which modern reckoning puts near a hundred and thirty pounds troy — a mass, Ellicott observes dryly, obviously more suited for the head of a big idol than of a man. Hence his two proposals for the closing clause, 'and it was set upon David's head': either, with the Vulgate, that David made himself a crown out of it, or that the weighty freight of gold and jewels was held over the king's head by his attendants on the occasion of the capture, a ceremony rather than a wearing.

The wesleyan-arminian John Wesley attends to the same talent but weighs it in coin, glossing 'to weigh a talent' as 'or, to be worth a talent, that is, five thousand four hundred and seventy-five pounds' — the crown reckoned not by the strain it would put on a neck but by the treasure it represented. Adam Clarke, also wesleyan-arminian, fastens rather on the precious stones set in it, and passes on the Targum's strange embellishment: 'there was set in it a precious stone, worth a talent of gold; this was that magnetic stone that supported the woven gold in the air.' Clarke can only shrug at the fancy — 'What does he mean?' — leaving the rabbinic legend of a jewel that held the golden crown aloft by magnetism as a curiosity he will not pretend to explain.

Ellicott closes on the plunder that came with the crown, the 'exceeding much spoil,' comparing the continual boast of the Assyrian conquerors, 'spoils without number I carried off'; and he notes that the precious stones, in Samuel's account, are reckoned within the talent's weight. Whether the golden circlet was the diadem of the Ammonite king or the crown lifted from the brow of his idol Milcom, and whether David wore it or only received it held above him, the verse marks the completeness of Ammon's fall: its king's honour, its god's ornament, and its treasure alike pass into the hand of Israel's king.
3:
'And he brought out the people that were in it' — and the witnesses face the chapter's hardest verse, the fate of the captured Ammonites, where a single Hebrew letter divides savagery from servitude. The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott renders it without flinching: the people that were in it he brought out, and sawed with the saw, and with the iron threshing-drags, and with the axes — the harrowing implements of Isaiah 41:15. The verb 'sawed' is an old word found only here, and Ellicott notes that Samuel, by the change of one letter, reads instead 'set them in,' or 'among,' the saws — the very variant on which the milder reading has always rested, whether David put the Ammonites to the saw or set them to labour among such tools. Our Hebrew text of Chronicles, he adds, repeats the word 'saw' in the plural through a scribe's error, the two words differing by a single letter, while Samuel's account further adds that David made them pass through the brick-kiln, or 'Moloch's fire,' the furnace of 2 Kings 23:10.

Ellicott will not soften the plain sense he adopts. The clause 'even so dealt David' is literally 'and so David used to do' — these cruelties, he insists, were enacted again at the taking of every Ammonite city, and not once only. There needs no attempt to palliate such revolting savagery, he writes; yet he sets it firmly in its age, for according to the ideas of that age it was only a glorious revenge. As David treated Ammon, so would the Ammonites have treated Israel had the victory been theirs — a mercilessness he documents from their own recorded behaviour toward the Gileadites in Amos 1:13, and sets beside the like atrocities of the Assyrian conquerors in Hosea 10:14 and of the Babylonians in Psalms 137:7.

The wesleyan-arminian Adam Clarke, for his part, declines to expound the horror at all, referring the reader simply to his fuller notes on the parallel place at 2 Samuel 12:30-31, where the transaction is particularly explained. So the verse is left with its crux open. The reading Ellicott adopts makes it a scene of judicial torture, executed and re-executed upon a whole people; the one-letter variant he reports leaves room for the gentler construction, that David condemned the conquered to hard labour with saw and axe and kiln rather than to death by them. Either way the witnesses agree that the act was terrible, that the age reckoned it just retribution, and that Scripture records it without gilding — a dark stroke set down in the reign of the man after God's own heart.
5:
No verse in the chapter has troubled harmonists more, for it flatly says that Elhanan the son of Jair slew Lahmi the brother of Goliath the Gittite, while the parallel at 2 Samuel 21:19 reads, in the Hebrew and the Septuagint alike, that Elhanan the son of Jaare-oregim the Bethlehemite slew Goliath the Gittite himself — the very champion whom 1 Samuel 17 gives to David. The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott lays out the whole tangle with unusual candour, canvassing three ways through it. First, there are good critics who take Samuel's text as it stands and see in it a proof that popular tradition fluctuated between David and the lesser hero Elhanan as the slayer of Goliath — an uncertainty faithfully preserved by the compiler of Samuel, who gives one account of the duel and this other of Elhanan. Second, other and not less competent scholars hold that Samuel's text should be corrected from the Chronicles: the strange name Jaare-oregim means 'forests of weavers,' an absurdity that looks like corruption, and there is no reason why 'Lahmi' should not be a real personal name; Ellicott presses that the Chronicler has shown no signs of arbitrary alteration and demonstrably possessed sources besides Samuel, so his reading may rest on genuine evidence rather than on mere harmonizing.

Third, Ellicott floats his own bolder conjecture: that 'Elhanan' may itself be another, and in fact the original, name of David — the appellative David, 'the beloved,' having gradually supplanted the older name in popular memory, just as Solomon was first named Jedidiah, and just as double names abound, Jehoahaz-Shallum, Mattaniah-Zedekiah, perhaps Uzziah-Azariah. Since 'Jaare' in Hebrew writing is an easy corruption of 'Jesse,' the original of 2 Samuel 21:19 may have run, 'And Elhanan the son of Jesse the Bethlehemite slew Goliath' — in which case, he grants, the Chronicles reading before us must be reckoned an unsuccessful emendation, made by the compiler whose work the Chronicler followed. So the same witness holds open at once the harmonizing solution and the possibility that Chronicles is itself the mistaken text.

The wesleyan-arminian Adam Clarke, referring the reader to his note on 2 Samuel 21:19, brings forward the ancient Jewish tradition that resolves the crux by identification. The Targum, he reports, expands the verse into a portrait of the king himself: 'David, the son of Jesse, a pious man, who rose at midnight to sing praises to God, slew Lachmi the brother of Goliath the Gittite, the same day on which he slew Goliath the Gittite, whose spear-staff was like a weaver's beam' — so that in the rabbinic reading Elhanan simply is David, and the man he here kills is not Goliath but Goliath's brother Lahmi, on the same day as the famous duel. The witnesses genuinely divide, and honestly so: whether Chronicles preserves the true reading against a corrupted Samuel, or Samuel preserves an older and fluctuating tradition that Chronicles has smoothed away, or 'Elhanan' is a lost name of David himself — they leave the knot half-tied, agreeing only that the difference between the two texts is real, that it turns on a few easily confused Hebrew letters, and that the Chronicler, here as throughout, writes with the honour of David and the house of Israel in view.
6:
The catalogue of giants reaches its most memorable prodigy — the man of great stature at Gath whose fingers and toes were four and twenty, six on each hand and six on each foot — and the two witnesses at hand treat it with a textual scholar's precision and a moralist's economy. The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott notes that Samuel gives a slightly different form of the opening phrase, 'a man of great stature,' or literally 'a man of measure,' and that in the count of the digits the Authorised Version here agrees with the Hebrew text of Samuel, while the Hebrew of Chronicles is abridged, reading tersely 'and his digits six and six — twenty and four.' The polydactyl monster, four-and-twenty fingers and toes where other men have twenty, is thus the same figure in both records, only more compactly told by the Chronicler, whose habit throughout this section is to prune the fuller narrative of Samuel to its bare bones. As for his descent, the phrase 'was the son of the giant' means, Ellicott explains, that he was born to the Rephaite — not sired by a single individual named 'the giant,' but sprung from the clan so named, the tribe of Rephaim to which all these Gath champions belonged.

The wesleyan-arminian Adam Clarke adds only the barest note, sending the reader to his fuller comment on the parallel at 2 Samuel 21:20 for the extra fingers and toes. Yet the very restraint of both men is instructive set against the wonder they record. The puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry, surveying the whole run of these combats, had already reduced just such a prodigy to nothing: what will a finger more on each hand do, he asks, or a toe more on each foot, in contest with Omnipotence? The six-fingered giant of Gath, for all his bulk and his surplus digits, is no match for the servants of David who have God on their side; the extra members that made him a marvel could not make him a victor. The witnesses do not divide. They leave the reader a figure at once grotesque and pathetic — the last flowering of the Rephaite stock, monstrous in body and defiant in spirit, yet doomed to fall like his kinsmen before a God who takes pleasure in abasing every lofty thing that lifts itself against his Israel.
8:
The section closes with a summary line — these were born unto the giant in Gath, and fell by the hand of David and by the hand of his servants — and the witnesses read both the sentence and the silences around it for what they reveal of the Chronicler's purpose. The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott attends first to the grammar: the opening word 'these' renders a rare Hebrew form found eight times in the Pentateuch with the article and here alone without it, perhaps a transcription error, where Samuel reads 'these four.' That number betrays a gap, for the Chronicler has in fact omitted one of the giants recorded in the fuller list, as his own truncated account back at 1 Chronicles 20:4 already showed. 'The giant' here again means 'the Rephaite,' the clan or tribe of Rephaim; and Ellicott is careful to add that these champions need not have been brothers, but only members of one gigantic stock.

The wesleyan-arminian Adam Clarke confirms the descent from the versions: they were born 'to that Rapha in Gath, or to Arapha,' as the Vulgate, Septuagint, and Chaldee render the phrase 'unto the giant.' But Clarke's deeper interest is the Chronicler's method of omission, which binds this closing verse back to the chapter's opening. As the compiler passed silently over David's sin with Bathsheba at 1 Chronicles 20:1, so here he passes by the incest of Amnon with his sister Tamar, the rebellion of Absalom, and all their awful consequences — matters which, in strict order, should have preceded the fourth verse. These facts could not have been unknown to him, Clarke insists, for they were notorious to all; but the compiler saw that they were already amply detailed in books accredited among the people, and that they were such as no friend to piety and humanity could delight to repeat. On those grounds, Clarke urges, the reader will give him credit for the omission rather than charge him with ignorance or evasion.

So the two witnesses close the chapter as they will not let the reader forget it began — with a Chronicler who records the triumphs of David and his servants over the last of the giants in full, while drawing a merciful veil over the king's private sins and his house's tragedies. The bones of the Rephaim fall by David's hand and are numbered one by one; the scandals of David's house are quietly left to the older record, that this history may keep its eyes fixed on the mercy and the victory God gave to Israel through his anointed king.
